## OQS Technical Steering Committee

### PQCA license scan (issue [#283](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/283))

Hart reported that the PQCA license scan found no major issues — the only recommendation is to ensure SPDX headers are present on OQS-authored files. Douglas noted that liboqs has CI tooling that already checks SPDX headers on files the project writes, with upstream-imported files excluded; the group agreed to revisit that script to confirm coverage.

### Conferences and outreach (issue [#276](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/276))

Rodrigo updated the venues ranking on the issue (building on the previous discussion) and asked TSC members to weigh in. He has already submitted talk proposals to the PKI Consortium and OpenSSL Conference, in both cases on the theme of "OQS in the era of published post-quantum standards" — continued mission, lessons learned, and future work.

On [Open Source Summit Europe](https://events.linuxfoundation.org/open-source-summit-europe/), Rodrigo hadn't been able to find the CFP page; Hart posted links to the [event page](https://events.linuxfoundation.org/open-source-summit-europe/) and the [CFP](https://events.linuxfoundation.org/open-source-summit-europe/program/cfp/) in chat, and confirmed there is an open CFP closing at the end of June. Hart noted that he is not on the LF Events deciding committee but offered to help structure proposals.

On travel funding: each LF event CFP has its own travel funding application. Broader travel support across multiple conferences is handled case-by-case and requires PQCA board approval. Rodrigo's company covers his own travel for legal reasons, so PQCA funding could potentially support a second attendee at conferences Rodrigo is already going to.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (TSC members):** Review Rodrigo's updated venue ranking on issue [#276](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/276).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Submit an Open Source Summit Europe proposal via the standard CFP before end of June.

## liboqs

### 0.16.0 release planning

Douglas summarized the remaining items for 0.16.0: the HQC update (PR [#2407](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2407)), the FrodoKEM value barrier (PR [#2431](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2431)), and the weekly build failures (issue [#2436](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2436)). His hope is to wrap these up by the end of this week, with a release candidate to follow — acknowledging the group has set this target before.

### HQC update (PR [#2407](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2407))

Bruce reported that the main outstanding item is a workaround in the SHA3-512 incremental API: when the official HQC implementation's FIPS 202 module was replaced directly with the liboqs SHA-3 implementation, the CI pipeline showed intermittent test failures. Replacing the incremental API calls with non-incremental calls makes the failures go away. Bruce has a fix that is correct but is still investigating whether it incurs any performance cost relative to the incremental API.

Michael observed that since this is a SHA-3 bug affecting every user of SHA-3 (not just HQC), the fix should be split into a separate PR. Bruce agreed. With the HQC team joining the next status meeting, the group felt splitting the SHA-3 fix out also makes a better first impression.

### HQC team engagement

Bruce and Rodrigo reported on outreach to the HQC team. The team has responded to Bruce's email and will join the May 26 status meeting; Rodrigo also spoke with Loïc (lead maintainer of the upstream repo) in person at Eurocrypt. The team is currently focused on changes required by the near-final FIPS 207 IPD, but they are interested in collaborating with OQS. Rodrigo noted that Loïc was somewhat out of date on OQS's scope — still thinking of it primarily as pqclean — so the OQS side should be prepared to explain the integration process in detail next week.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all):** Prepare to walk the HQC team through the OQS integration process at the May 26 status meeting; expect that meeting to focus largely on this discussion.

Douglas has reviewed and approved the PR. The group is happy to land it with a single review if needed, but additional reviewers are welcome. Once merged, Douglas will issue the corresponding security advisory.

Douglas flagged the failing weekly builds and has a note to investigate.

### LMS NIST ACVP test vectors (PR [#2435](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2435))

Norm posted PR [#2435](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2435) in the chat, which adds NIST ACVP server test vectors for LMS — a follow-up to the levels 0/1 work that landed last week, addressing earlier interop comments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (reviewers):** Review PR [#2435](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2435).

### Algorithm support level responses

Following the merge of the support levels table, Rodrigo contacted each upstream algorithm team. Responses received so far: LMS, NTRU Prime, UOV, MQOM, XMSS, SLH-DSA, CROSS, MAYO, SNOVA, and NTRU. All responses are either "actively maintained" or "best effort" — no team has declared no interest. Four teams have not yet responded; Basil noted that Rodrigo's email to the MAYO team went to spam, so Rodrigo will resend if needed.

Michael suggested that rather than one PR per algorithm, Rodrigo bundle the responses into a single PR (easier for reviewers); Rodrigo agreed. The group also agreed that the PRs should record the outcome rather than the raw email contents, unless any specific text is worth archiving publicly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Open a PR (or grouped PRs) updating the algorithm support levels table with the responses received; resend to the four non-responding teams (including a re-send to MAYO) after a week.

### CROSS deprecation (NIST round-2 → round-3)

NIST has narrowed the on-ramp signature candidates from 14 (originally 16) down to 9; CROSS is the one round-2 candidate that did not advance. Per OQS practice, CROSS would be flagged for deprecation in 0.16 and removal in 0.17. Douglas cautioned against sending a deprecation notice to the CROSS team immediately after the NIST rejection, especially given they had just responded as "actively maintained." Convention is to first ask the team about alternative standardization paths (e.g., ISO, CFRG). The group agreed to hold off on the deprecation announcement for 0.16 and revisit in a month or two once the CROSS team has settled on their plans.

### Round-3 on-ramp signature scheme inclusion

Of the 9 round-3 on-ramp signature schemes, 4 are not yet in liboqs. Rodrigo proposed making 0.17 the release that includes all round-3 algorithms — an ambitious target but one he wants to pursue. SQIsign already has a draft PR (Basil leading) and FAAEST has had prior integration attempts. Rodrigo will create tracking issues for the remaining schemes.

Basil pointed out that NIST allows tweaks to round-3 submissions, so the current versions of these algorithms may change; submission teams interested in contributing will likely want to contribute the post-tweak round-3 version rather than the current code. Round-3 final submissions are due August 14. With 0.17 likely landing around October/November, the timing should still work to include at least some of them. Rodrigo will hold off on contacting the round-3 teams until around the TSC meeting (first week of June) to avoid adding to their workload during the tweak window.

Rodrigo also raised the idea of using LF Mentorship Program (autumn cohort) slots to help integrate algorithms; the group agreed this is a TSC-level discussion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Open tracking issues for the round-3 on-ramp signature schemes not yet in liboqs; reach out to those teams around the June TSC meeting.

## Language wrappers

### liboqs-Python

Douglas spent time last week cleaning up open issues and PRs on liboqs-Python and has cleared most of the backlog. One promising external contributor has been engaged on a feature for caching keying material from stateful hash-based signatures to reduce CI runtime. The repository is essentially ready to match the liboqs 0.16.0 release.

A little organizational work remains to transfer ownership of the PyPI namespace via the Linux Foundation.

### PyPI namespace concern

Michael asked what level of long-term support OQS is committing to by publishing an official Python package, drawing a parallel to past binary-distribution debates and noting that liboqs-Rust no longer has an active team behind it. Douglas's view is that this is a relatively lightweight thing to package — release would be automated — and that the project can revisit if the maintenance burden grows.

Hart strongly endorsed owning the namespace: unaffiliated parties publishing packages under your project's name on package registries is a known malware-injection vector (he has seen this happen repeatedly on NPM); even a deprecated/placeholder package owned by the project is preferable to leaving the slot open.

Douglas then noticed that on May 15, someone *not on the project* — "Kelly D. Behrendt" — pushed a release of liboqs-python to PyPI. Hart confirmed in chat that he and Ry had also been trying to figure out who this contributor was on Sunday. Hart recommended first reaching out politely to ask their intent; the vast majority of such cases are well-intentioned. If that fails, the situation can be escalated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Contact the PyPI user who pushed the May 15 liboqs-python release to understand their relationship to the project, then proceed with the PyPI ownership transfer through the Linux Foundation.

## Next meetings

Next OQS status meeting: Tuesday, May 26, 2026, at 10:00 AM US Eastern — expected to focus on the HQC team joining and walking through the OQS integration process. Next OQS TSC meeting: Tuesday, June 2, 2026, at 12:30 PM US Eastern.
